title: Platform Agents & Structured Output
subtitle: Format creative drafts for Twitter, Instagram, and other platforms. Handle failures gracefully with return_exceptions.

concept.heading: Why platform-specific agents?
concept.body:
A creative draft is platform-agnostic. Twitter needs ≤280 chars and 2-4 hashtags. Instagram needs a hook in the first 2 lines and 15-20 hashtags. YouTube needs a thumbnail hook and chapter timestamps. LinkedIn needs professional tone.

A single agent formatting for all platforms either compromises all of them or writes the lowest common denominator. Dedicated platform agents know exactly what their platform requires.

callouts.title: 280 chars is a hard API limit
callouts.body: Twitter's API rejects posts over 280 characters. It's not a style guideline — it's a hard constraint. A post that passes QA at 285 chars will fail at publish time. The platform agent must enforce this.

callouts.title: Platform agents produce structured output
callouts.body: Each platform agent returns a specific JSON schema. Twitter returns {main_tweet, thread_tweets, hashtags, char_count}. Instagram returns {caption_hook, full_caption, hashtags, media_suggestion}. The QA agent knows exactly which fields to score.

endQuiz.stem: A creative draft is 320 characters. The Twitter platform agent receives it. What should it do?
options: Truncate to 280 chars silently
Raise an exception and abort
Return the post with needs_revision=true and specific cut instructions
Publish it anyway — Twitter auto-truncates
endQuiz.explanation: Return with a flag so QA catches it. Silent truncation destroys the punchline. Raising an exception fails the whole platform orchestrator. needs_revision=true with cut instructions lets QA trigger a targeted revision.

endQuiz.stem: Why do Twitter and Instagram have different hashtag counts (2-4 vs 15-20)?
options: Instagram has a higher character limit
Different discovery algorithms — Instagram surfaces posts to hashtag followers at scale; Twitter's hashtag impact plateaus after 4
It's just convention, no technical reason
Twitter's API rejects more than 4 hashtags
endQuiz.explanation: Instagram's algorithm uses hashtags for discovery at scale — 15-20 maximises reach across topic communities. Twitter's algorithm de-emphasises posts with many hashtags after 4, treating them as spam-like.

lessons.title: The asyncio.gather Failure Problem

concept.heading: One failure kills all results
concept.body:
The platform orchestrator runs 5 platform agents simultaneously via `asyncio.gather(*tasks)`. By default, if Instagram's agent raises an exception (rate limit, API error), `asyncio.gather` propagates that exception immediately — discarding ALL already-completed results.

Twitter finished at second 1. Instagram failed at second 2. Default behaviour: you lose the Twitter result.

callouts.title: asyncio.gather default: first exception kills all
callouts.body: asyncio.gather(*tasks) propagates the first exception raised by any task. The 4 tasks that succeeded are gone. You get 0 results instead of 4.

callouts.title: return_exceptions=True: exceptions as values
callouts.body: asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True) returns exceptions as regular values in the results list. You check isinstance(r, Exception) and handle each independently. The 4 successes survive the 1 failure.

todos.marker:
???   # ⚠ TODO: One argument prevents ONE failing agent from
              # crashing the ENTIRE orchestrator.
              # Hint: Instagram is rate-limited. Twitter works fine.
              # Without this, asyncio.gather raises on the first exception —
              # all other completed results are DISCARDED.
todos.whyItMatters: Without this, Instagram failing at second 2 discards Twitter results that finished at second 1. You get 0 posts instead of 4. return_exceptions=True makes failures first-class values you can inspect and handle per-platform.

midQuiz.stem: asyncio.gather runs 5 tasks. Task 3 raises RateLimitError. Without return_exceptions=True, you get:
options: Results from tasks 1, 2, 4, 5 plus the exception from task 3
RateLimitError raised — results from tasks 1, 2, 4, 5 are discarded
None — all tasks are cancelled
Results from tasks 1, 2 only (tasks before the failure)
midQuiz.explanation: asyncio.gather propagates the first exception by default. Tasks that already completed have their results discarded. You get the exception, nothing else.

completeCode.code:
TWITTER_SYSTEM = """
Format a creative draft for Twitter.
Constraints:
- main_tweet <= 280 characters (HARD LIMIT — Twitter API rejects otherwise)
- 2-4 hashtags maximum
- thread: 3-5 follow-up tweets if content warrants
If main_tweet > 280 chars: set needs_revision=True with specific cut instructions.
Output JSON: {main_tweet, thread_tweets, hashtags, char_count, needs_revision, revision_note}
"""

INSTAGRAM_SYSTEM = """
Format a creative draft for Instagram.
Constraints:
- First line must be a hook (Instagram shows only first 1-2 lines)
- 15-20 hashtags (algorithm benefit)
- Media suggestion required
Output JSON: {caption_hook, full_caption, hashtags, media_suggestion}
"""

async def twitter_agent(draft: dict) -> list[dict]:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024,
        system=TWITTER_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Format: {json.dumps(draft)}"}]
    )
    post = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)
    post["platform"] = "twitter"
    return [post]

async def instagram_agent(draft: dict) -> list[dict]:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024,
        system=INSTAGRAM_SYSTEM,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Format: {json.dumps(draft)}"}]
    )
    post = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)
    post["platform"] = "instagram"
    return [post]

PLATFORM_AGENTS = {"twitter": twitter_agent, "instagram": instagram_agent}

async def platform_orchestrator_node(state: PipelineState) -> dict:
    tasks = [PLATFORM_AGENTS[p](d)
             for d in state["creative_drafts"]
             for p in d.get("target_platforms", [])
             if p in PLATFORM_AGENTS]
    
    results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    
    posts, errors = [], []
    for r in results:
        if isinstance(r, Exception):
            errors.append(str(r))
        else:
            posts.extend(r)
    return {"platform_posts": posts}

annotations.text: return_exceptions=True — the production fix for partial failure
isinstance(result, Exception) — check each result individually, not the batch

lessons.pyodideCode:
import asyncio

async def mock_twitter_agent(draft):
    """Succeeds — returns formatted tweet"""
    return [{"platform": "twitter", "main_tweet": f"IPL fever hits Mumbai homes! {draft.get('trend_hashtag', '#IPL2025')}",
             "char_count": 48, "hashtags": ["#IPL2025", "#MumbaiHomes"]}]

async def mock_instagram_agent(draft):
    """Simulates a rate limit failure"""
    raise Exception("Instagram API: Rate limit exceeded (429)")

async def mock_youtube_agent(draft):
    return [{"platform": "youtube", "title": "Mumbai Real Estate 2025: What You Need To Know"}]

draft = {"trend_hashtag": "#IPL2025", "content": "IPL fever hits Mumbai",
         "target_platforms": ["twitter", "instagram", "youtube"]}

PLATFORM_AGENTS = {
    "twitter": mock_twitter_agent,
    "instagram": mock_instagram_agent,  # will fail
    "youtube": mock_youtube_agent
}

async def run_orchestrator(use_return_exceptions: bool):
    tasks = [PLATFORM_AGENTS[p](draft) for p in draft["target_platforms"]]
    try:
        if use_return_exceptions:
            results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
            posts, errors = [], []
            for r in results:
                if isinstance(r, Exception):
                    errors.append(str(r))
                    print(f"  Non-fatal error: {r}")
                else:
                    posts.extend(r)
            print(f"  Result: {len(posts)} posts, {len(errors)} errors")
            return posts
        else:
            results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)  # no return_exceptions
            return [post for r in results for post in r]
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"  FATAL: {e} — ALL results lost")
        return []

print("Without return_exceptions=True:")
result1 = asyncio.run(run_orchestrator(False))
print(f"  Posts saved: {len(result1)}\n")

print("With return_exceptions=True:")
result2 = asyncio.run(run_orchestrator(True))
print(f"  Posts saved: {len(result2)}")

endQuiz.stem: True or False: `return_exceptions=True` silently swallows exceptions so your code never sees them.
endQuiz.explanation: False — return_exceptions=True returns exceptions as first-class values in the results list. Your code checks isinstance(r, Exception) to detect and log each one. Nothing is silently hidden — you get explicit per-task control.

endQuiz.stem: The platform orchestrator has a 180s timeout and it fires. What should happen?
options: Crash the entire pipeline run
Retry all platform agents immediately
Return whatever posts completed before the timeout, log the timeout as a non-fatal error
Ask the user what to do
endQuiz.explanation: Partial success is better than total failure. Return completed posts, log the timeout. The RetryPolicy (Module 10) handles the orchestrator-level retry with exponential backoff.

concept.heading: Every platform agent has a strict schema
concept.body:
Platform agents must return JSON matching a specific schema. The QA agent knows this schema and scores against it. The publisher uses it to format the final post.

If twitter_agent returns `tweet_text` instead of `main_tweet`, the QA agent silently gets None for char_count and passes a post that may be too long.

callouts.title: Validate at the boundary
callouts.body: In production, platform agents validate their JSON output against a Pydantic model before returning. This catches schema drift immediately rather than at QA or publish time.

codeExample.code:
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional

class TwitterPost(BaseModel):
    main_tweet: str
    thread_tweets: list[str] = []
    hashtags: list[str]
    char_count: int
    needs_revision: bool = False
    revision_note: Optional[str] = None

async def twitter_agent(draft: dict) -> list[dict]:
    resp = client.messages.create(...)
    raw = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)
    post = TwitterPost(**raw)  # validates schema immediately
    return [post.model_dump()]

endQuiz.stem: A platform agent returns {'tweet': 'text', 'tags': [...]} instead of {'main_tweet': 'text', 'hashtags': [...]}. When does this fail?
options: Immediately — LangGraph validates schemas
At the QA agent — it reads main_tweet and gets None, silently
At publish time — Twitter API rejects the schema
Never — dicts accept any keys
endQuiz.explanation: Without Pydantic validation, the wrong keys flow silently through the pipeline. QA reads main_tweet and gets None (KeyError or None), potentially passing or failing posts incorrectly. The bug manifests far from the source.
